one_line_takeaway: A PRISMA-guided review-of-reviews that distills 27 survey/review articles (2021-2024) into a taxonomy of AI-based ransomware detection, mitigation, and open challenges, but performs no original experiments and its own quantitative output is limited to the literature-search/inclusion counts, not any detection-accuracy figures.
key_finding: The synthesized literature converges on hybrid AI models (combining static and dynamic/behavioral analysis) and pre-encryption/early-detection strategies (anomaly detection, entropy-based monitoring, real-time isolation) as the dominant effective paradigm for ransomware defense across the 27 reviewed studies. The authors' own bibliometric result is the composition of that corpus: identification yielded 315 (IEEE Xplore), 633 (ACM), 1,202 (ScienceDirect), 1,796 (SpringerLink), 622 (Wiley), and 17 (MDPI) candidate articles; after eligibility filtering and inclusion, 27 articles were retained, distributed as 6 (2021), 7 (2022), 7 (2023), 7 (2024), with none meeting criteria from 2020. Recurring cross-cutting challenges identified in the synthesis are adversarial ML evasion of detectors, scarce/unrepresentative (synthetic or sandbox-generated) datasets, high false-positive rates, and scalability limits when moving from academic studies to production-scale data volumes.
core_contribution: A tertiary 'review of reviews' on AI (ML/DL) for ransomware defense. Using a PRISMA-guided protocol, the authors searched IEEE Xplore, ACM, ScienceDirect, SpringerLink, Wiley, and MDPI (Scopus/Web of Science excluded due to the authors' institutional access limits) for review/survey articles (2020-2024) at the intersection of 'Ransomware' and 'AI'/'Artificial Intelligence'. After applying eligibility criteria (full-text access, review/survey format, topical relevance, and Q1/Q2 journal ranking), they retained 27 review/survey articles and synthesized them into a taxonomy covering AI's role in ransomware detection/prevention, mitigation, challenges, and future research directions. The paper's actual output is this synthesis and taxonomy (Sections 4-8), not any new experiment, model, or dataset.
threat_model: Not an original threat model proposed by the authors; the paper synthesizes the threat landscape as described across the 27 reviewed studies. It characterizes ransomware via a six-stage attack workflow (reconnaissance, delivery, execution, persistence, encryption, extortion), categorizes ransomware by function (crypto vs. locker), target (individual vs. 'Big Game Hunting' organizational targets), and deployment (manual via stolen RDP credentials vs. automated/botnet), and discusses the Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS) business model and double-extortion tactics. On the defender-adversary dynamic, it discusses adversarial machine learning (evasion of ML/DL detectors via crafted inputs) and evasion techniques such as fileless/PowerShell-based payload delivery and polymorphic/metamorphic obfuscation, framed as an ongoing 'arms race' -- all attributed to the underlying reviewed literature rather than newly modeled by this paper.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Discussion, Section 8): many of the underlying reviewed studies rely on synthetic or sandbox-generated datasets that may not reflect real-world ransomware behavior; public repositories used in the underlying literature often lack standardized labeling, causing evaluation inconsistencies; these factors introduce potential bias and reduce generalizability to novel ransomware variants; legal/ethical barriers restrict access to diverse real-world data, hindering reproducibility. Methodological limitations evident from the text itself: database coverage excludes Scopus and Web of Science specifically because of the authors' own institutional access restrictions (a convenience/access-driven rather than principled exclusion); Section 3.3 and Section 3.4 give internally inconsistent intermediate counts (Section 3.3 implies totals of 26 with 4 MDPI articles and 8 in 2024, while Section 3.4 states the final corpus is 27 with 5 MDPI articles and 7 in 2024) -- the paper's own final, consistently-used figure is 27. As a tertiary review of reviews, the paper inherits and does not independently verify any of the quantitative claims (accuracy, false-positive rates, attack statistics) reported in its source reviews.

quantitative_results: Database search yield (Identification stage): IEEE Xplore 315, ACM 633, ScienceDirect 1,202, SpringerLink 1,796, Wiley 622, MDPI 17 candidate articles
Final included corpus: 27 review/survey articles after eligibility screening
Year distribution of the 27 included articles: 6 in 2021, 7 in 2022, 7 in 2023, 7 in 2024; 0 in 2020
Per-database composition of final corpus (Section 3.4): 7 from ScienceDirect, 5 from IEEE Xplore, 4 from ACM Digital Library, 4 from SpringerLink, 5 from MDPI, 2 from Wiley Online Library
